ontology.foodex2_description: Sago-based traditional dessert (steamed, wrapped in banana leaf)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for abok abok sago; A0CKP (starchy roots and tubers products) used as closest defensible anchor for sago-derived confection.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for abok abok sago or equivalent traditional Malay sago dessert.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of abok abok sago is approximately one piece (about 60 g), as commonly sold at Singaporean hawker stalls and kuih counters.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — Abok Abok Sago
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Abok Abok Sago (cr_id F11090001). Saturated fat and sugar values estimated from coconut milk and palm sugar composition using HPB ingredient data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-reference, as these sub-fractions are not individually reported in the HPB entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Sago, steamed
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for abok abok sago found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 based on steamed sago pudding / sago-based dessert analogues reported in Atkinson FS et al. (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Sago starch GI range reported as 70–85 for steamed preparations; conservative lower bound of 70 applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 30.0) / 100 = 21.0, based on 60 g serving containing 30.0 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 396 mg/100 g combined with high saturated fat from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit abok abok sago to occasional small portions (≤60 g) due to sodium at 396 mg/100 g approaching 17% of the 2,300 mg daily limit per serving, compounded by high saturated fat (≈14.8 g/100 g) which promotes arterial stiffness and elevates cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI sago starch (GI ≈70) with 50 g carbohydrate and estimated 18 g sugar per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or very strictly limit abok abok sago because the high-GI sago starch (GI ≈70) combined with 50 g carbohydrate and approximately 18 g sugar per 100 g produces rapid postprandial glucose elevation; even a single 60 g piece delivers approximately 30 g available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of 21.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat from coconut milk (≈14.8 g/100 g) raising LDL cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat abok abok sago as an occasional indulgence only, as the estimated saturated fat of approximately 14.8 g/100 g — predominantly from coconut milk — significantly raises LDL cholesterol and contributes to atherosclerotic risk when consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (≈14.8 g/100 g) from coconut milk promoting pro-inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of abok abok sago because the high saturated fat content from coconut milk (≈14.8 g/100 g) promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, and the coconut oil anti-inflammatory carve-out does not apply to coconut milk in this preparation context.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Abok abok sago is a traditional Malay kuih (bite-sized dessert) popular in Singapore and Malaysia, made from tapioca sago pearls, palm sugar (gula melaka), grated coconut, and coconut milk, steamed and wrapped in banana leaves. The banana leaf imparts a subtle herbal fragrance during steaming. The finished kuih has a soft, slightly chewy, translucent texture from the sago, a rich sweetness from the palm sugar, and a creamy, mildly savoury undertone from the grated coconut and coconut milk. It is commonly found at Singaporean hawker centres, wet markets, and Malay kuih stalls, particularly during festive occasions such as Hari Raya. Nutritionally, abok abok sago is energy-dense at 372 kcal/100 g, with a high carbohydrate load (50 g/100 g), high fat content predominantly from saturated coconut fat (≈14.8 g/100 g), moderate dietary fibre (3.8 g/100 g), and elevated sodium (396 mg/100 g). The sago base has a high glycaemic index (estimated GI ≈70), making this dessert unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease except in very small occasional portions. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11090001, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and sugar sub-fractions estimated from coconut milk and palm sugar composition using HPB ingredient data cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI estimated from peer-reviewed analogous sago dessert data (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). All mandatory multilingual names present for Singaporean cuisine category.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (14.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.8 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.3 g/100 g) are estimated from coconut milk and grated coconut composition using HPB ingredient data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (coconut milk, raw, USDA NDB 12117); these sub-fractions are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish. 2. Sugar (18.0 g/100 g) is estimated from palm sugar content in the recipe; not directly reported in the HPB entry. 3. potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this traditional preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a plant-based food containing no animal-derived ingredients). 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food with no animal products). 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived or fortified ingredients). 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). 8. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on steamed sago dessert data from Atkinson et al. (2008); direct GI measurement for abok abok sago is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 9. The input record listed 'high_fibre' as a dietary tag; this is borderline — dietary fibre at 3.8 g/100 g meets the Singapore HPB threshold for high fibre (≥3 g/100 g for solid foods) and has been retained. 10. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry exists for abok abok sago in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2026.
